medications.indication_rationale: Prompt oral antibiotics targeting streptococci/staphylococci treat cellulitis and prevent spread in a compromised limb; the choice/dose and confirmation (and DVT exclusion) should be made at in-person assessment rather than blind over telehealth. The source prescribed flucloxacillin 500 mg QID for 10 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: flucloxacillin 500 mg orally four times daily for 10 days (as prescribed in the source in-person encounter; confirm choice/allergy at assessment)
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm penicillin allergy status before prescribing; choose an alternative if allergic
Complete the full course even if the redness fades early

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Community nursing for regular lower-limb skin/wound monitoring, erythema surveillance and lymphoedema skin care

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Tight compression garment over the acutely inflamed area
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Compression over acute painful cellulitis worsens pain and is contraindicated until the inflammation settles; resume once the infection resolves.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Self-managing with leftover home antibiotics without assessment
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Risks wrong antibiotic/dose, masks a possible DVT, and delays proper assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the leg elevated above hip level when resting, and watch the marked edge of the redness for spread; do not apply tight compression over the inflamed area.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation reduces fluid pressure and monitoring detects spread early; compression over acute inflammation is harmful.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Practise meticulous daily skin care (wash with pH-neutral soap, apply emollient, avoid scratching) once the acute infection settles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintaining the skin barrier reduces recurrent cellulitis in a lymphoedematous limb.

patient_education_points.point: The chronic swelling in your leg has damaged the skin barrier, and a skin infection (cellulitis) has set in - shown by the hot, red, painful patch and mild fever. This needs prompt antibiotics; because the leg is swollen and vulnerable, it should be examined in person today or tomorrow to confirm it, rule out a clot, and choose the right antibiotic.

Take the prescribed antibiotics exactly on time and finish the whole course even if the redness fades. Keep the leg elevated, and don't put a tight compression stocking over the painful red area until it settles.

Go to the emergency department immediately if the redness spreads quickly beyond the marked edge, you develop a high spiking fever with shaking chills, or you start vomiting or feel very unwell - these can mean the infection is spreading into the bloodstream.
Once healed, daily gentle skin care and your compression garment will help prevent future infections.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person assessment (urgent, same day to within 24 hours) for confirmation/antibiotics and DVT exclusion; GP review at 48 hours to confirm the redness is retreating; community nursing skin monitoring
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to antibiotics (retreating erythema at the marked edge), exclusion of DVT, systemic features, skin integrity and lymphoedema skin care, and recurrence-prevention strategies
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Rapidly spreading redness, rigors/high fever, or vomiting/systemic upset - attend the emergency department immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of cellulitis complicating chronic lymphoedema (infection-prone limb)
Prompt in-person assessment for confirmation, antibiotics, and DVT exclusion
Appropriate oral antibiotics with full-course advice (choice confirmed in person)
Avoid tight compression over the acute inflammation; elevate the limb; skin care
Sepsis/spread safety-netting (spreading redness, rigors, vomiting -> ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent same-day or within-24-hour in-person assessment are acceptable given systemic wellness; 'skin infection/cellulitis in the swollen leg' is an acceptable framing
If telehealth prescribing is available locally, oral antibiotics may be started with a clear plan for in-person review and DVT exclusion
errors_of_omission: Not recognising cellulitis / not arranging antibiotics
Not advising against tight compression over the acute area
errors_of_commission: Over-anchoring on DVT and arranging only an ultrasound while ignoring the infection
Advising tight compression over the inflamed area
Reassuring without any antibiotics or assessment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising cellulitis on a lymphoedematous limb and arranging prompt antibiotics with in-person assessment/DVT exclusion, (2) avoiding tight compression over the acute area, and (3) sepsis/spread safety-netting. Ignoring the infection, over-anchoring on DVT, or omitting sepsis netting are failures.
